tipRules.tip: Sealock: The Senko's tail wiggles back and forth on slack line without any angler effort; its slow fall from high salt content makes it irresistible; casts like a rocket even weightless and skips easily under cover
Sealock: Fish the Senko on a weightless Texas rig around vegetation or target-casting to stumps/laydowns — totally weedless and the tail kicks like crazy
Sealock: Right before bass begin spawning, they focus on 'love' more than eating — the slow fall of a weightless Texas-rigged Senko is great at fooling them into biting

Sealock: Use a 1/4-oz weighted Texas rig Senko for heavy cover; lighter weight is always optimal — heavier weights cause it to sink too fast, taking away from its trademark horizontal fall; let it fall on slack line as most bites come on the fall

Sealock: Carolina-rigging Senkos with a monofilament leader causes the Senko to float and slowly wave back and forth throughout the retrieve — great for rocky points, shell beds, river bars, and bluff walls

knowledge.insight: Sealock: When the flipping/pitching bite gets tough, switch to a wacky-rigged Senko on a spinning rod with 8-lb test — fish it in the same areas (under docks, laydowns, boulders, grass lines); hook through the middle, make a long cast, let fall on slack line, pop the slack occasionally

Sealock: The Senko's weight makes it cast like a rocket even rigged weightless — critical for finesse fishing in clear water where long casts are essential; it skips underneath cover with just a wrist flick
